item.title: “Greater New York” Takes the Pulse of the City
item.description: Also: the megawatt hip-hop of Baby Keem, the buzzy period reimaginings of Scottish Ballet, the time-capsule documentary “With Hasan in Gaza,” and more.

item.title: Dean’s Is Not Your Average Pub
item.description: A new downtown restaurant from the team behind King serves proudly jolie-laide English classics, beautifully.
item.dc:creator: ByHelen Rosner

item.title: The Knicks: The Only Game in Town
item.description: The Knicks have made the N.B.A. Finals again and, as another home team instructs the city, “Ya gotta believe.”
item.dc:creator: ByDavid Remnick

item.title: The Happy Ending Romeo and Juliet Didn’t Get
item.description: This year’s Shakespeare in the Park production puts the star-crossed lovers on the U.S.-Mexico border. And there’s another twist—each performance ends with a real-life wedding.
item.dc:creator: ByMichael Schulman

item.title: Dusty Baker Plays the Long Game
item.description: In his lifetime, the M.L.B. manager has won multiple World Series, shared a joint with Jimi Hendrix, and co-invented the high five. Will he add Hall of Famer to the mix?
item.dc:creator: ByCharles Bethea

item.title: Leo Woodall Gets a Tune Up
item.description: The “White Lotus” actor plays a piano technician in his new movie, “Tuner.” At a real tuning session, in midtown, he tries to remember how to plink the strings.
item.dc:creator: ByJane Bua

item.title: Remembering Donald Newhouse
item.description: The newspaper visionary and owner of Condé Nast, who died last week, at the age of ninety-six, understood the value of editorial independence.
item.dc:creator: ByDavid Remnick

item.title: The World Cup According to Gianni Infantino
item.description: FIFA’s powerful president is remaking global soccer in his own image. Can the sport survive him?
item.dc:creator: BySam Knight

item.title: The Paperboy’s Secret
item.description: In boyhood, guilt was a constant companion. I stopped mentioning the quarters that Mr. Wood put into my pocket.
item.dc:creator: ByPeter Hessler

item.title: “Stories”
item.description: I believed that, when it came to words, everything was allowed.
item.dc:creator: ByAnnie Ernaux

item.title: “Firstborn Immigrant Daughter”
item.description: Your mother doesn’t want her daughter to be happy. Your mother wants her daughter to be impressive. And you tried, o!
item.dc:creator: ByTaiye Selasi

item.title: “The Twice-Widowed Khala Helai”
item.description: Fifty years and I dream of him still, fifty years and every story, every joke, every bit of news or gossip leads back to him still.
item.dc:creator: ByJamil Jan Kochai

item.title: “A Talent for Seeming”
item.description: Lust wasn’t her only sin. There was also an actor’s envy.
item.dc:creator: ByJonathan Franzen

item.title: The Tally
item.description: Honesty may be a one-sided contract with the world, but it is the only side that we can control.
item.dc:creator: ByAnne Enright

item.title: Love Omen
item.description: I was panicking a bit, thinking of a career, thinking that I should probably get one.
item.dc:creator: ByMiriam Toews

item.title: The Antagonist
item.description: I suddenly wondered what had happened to my mother, as if she were one of my characters.
item.dc:creator: ByHan Ong

item.title: Forward Into Foreignness
item.description: That’s how we did things in my family. Tabbouleh, hurling, helva, “Inshallah,” “godverdomme”—all of it was our culture.
item.dc:creator: ByJoseph O’Neill

item.title: Table Manners
item.description: At mealtimes, the people who had severed my ties to Trinidad were my only channel back to it.
item.dc:creator: ByAndré Alexis

item.title: Why the American Novel Refused to Grow Up
item.description: For the critic Leslie Fiedler, the country’s best and worst fiction was shaped by visions of escape from society—and therefore from maturity.
item.dc:creator: ByBecca Rothfeld

item.title: What Did “Lady Chatterley” Liberate?
item.description: Once outlawed as obscene, D. H. Lawrence’s novel was meant to heal the world’s sickness about sex. Instead, it mattered most as a legal milestone, a pop-culture shorthand, and a meme.
item.dc:creator: ByLouis Menand

item.title: Briefly Noted
item.description: “Look What You Made Me Do,” “Magadh,” “Adrift in the South,” and “The Story of Birds.”

item.title: The Star-Crossed Recluse Who Brought Astrology to the Masses
item.description: Linda Goodman argued that our destinies were written. But her own life took some unexpected turns.
item.dc:creator: ByRachel Syme

item.title: Maggie O’Farrell and the Art of Inventing the Past
item.description: Why read historical fiction? A new novel by the author of “Hamnet” offers one answer: because it’s fun.
item.dc:creator: ByKaty Waldman

item.title: “Power Ballad,” Reviewed: A Bromantic Conflict Over a Hit Song
item.description: In John Carney’s dramedy, a thwarted songwriter, played by Paul Rudd, crosses paths with a former boy-band star in search of new material.
item.dc:creator: ByRichard Brody

item.title: “How to Grow Old”
item.description: “Don’t fret about / the champagne-glass / neck. / Drink champagne.”
item.dc:creator: BySandra Cisneros

item.title: “Mirage”
item.description: “In this world of endless disjunctions and conjunctions, / you struggle to parse the zigzag flight of a butterfly.”
item.dc:creator: ByArthur Sze

item.title: Cartoon Caption Contest
item.description: Submit a caption, plus vote on other entries and finalists.

item.title: Cartoons from the Issue
item.description: Drawings from the June 8, 2026, magazine.

item.title: The Crossword: Tuesday, May 26, 2026
item.description: Today’s theme: Left speechless.
item.dc:creator: ByAdam Aaronson
